Pricing is published on the operator website (elderlycare.my) starting from RM 2,500 per month for a shared room — a starting figure that, in practice, varies with the resident's actual care needs and dependency level. Bed count and current room availability are not published online.

Kepong Baru is a dense residential pocket in north-Kuala Lumpur with good access to Hospital Sungai Buloh and the Damansara hospital cluster (KPJ Damansara, Sunway, ParkCity Medical Centre). The neighbourhood is tightly built — terrace-house lots, narrow back lanes — so families should ask on a visit how natural light, communal space, and any outdoor compound are handled, how many residents share a room, how many caregivers cover each shift, whether a registered nurse is on-site overnight or on-call, and what specific clinical procedures the home performs in-house versus refers out (PEG feeds, oxygen, wound dressings, urinary catheter changes). Confirm the RM 2,500 starting rate matches the actual care plan since add-ons can shift the monthly figure.

title: Procare Care Centre
longitude: Procare Care Centre is a care home located in Kuala Lumpur. The home accommodates up to 65 residents.
editorial_summary:
Procare Care Centre has been operating in Taman Yarl, Kuala Lumpur, since 1997 and is registered with the Ministry of Health (MOH) — a distinction held by relatively few private nursing homes in Malaysia. Based on Jalan Awan Dandan, it positions itself around post-stroke rehabilitation and high-dependency nursing care, and reportedly draws a meaningful share of admissions from hospital discharges and families managing complex medical conditions at home.

The clinical depth advertised is unusual for the segment. The operator states that an in-house doctor conducts daily consultations and that a resident physiotherapist runs daily sessions, with the team equipped to manage paralysis, coma states, PEG feeding, and dialysis-compatible needs. These are operator claims rather than third-party-verified facts: the main website is currently down, no licence number is published in the public materials beyond the MOH-registration label, and reviews are too thin to corroborate the staffing profile. The operator also reports more than 20 staff and 27 years of continuous operation.

Procare is worth shortlisting for families in the KL south area whose relative needs more medical capability than a typical residential nursing home offers — it is a candidate to verify on the visit, not a default first call. Pricing is not published, so call 03-79818522 or use the Facebook channel (PCCSB05) for a quote keyed to dependency level and room type. On the visit, ask for the MOH registration certificate by reference number, the named in-house doctor and their actual hours on site (full-day, ward-round only, or on-call labelled "daily"), the named physiotherapist and the typical session length per resident, the day and night RN-to-resident ratio, and how dialysis-compatible care is delivered in practice — own equipment, partnered centre, or transport-to-clinic.

title: Rumah Orang Tua Charis
editorial_summary:
Rumah Orang Tua Charis is a Christian welfare home in Taman Lucky, Kuala Lumpur, founded in 1988 by Reverend Teo How Ken and registered as a charitable organisation with the Registrar of Societies in 1993. The home accommodates 17 elderly residents and operates alongside a children's home under the same organisation. Weekly services are held at the affiliated Methodist Charis Community Care Center, and a social programme called the VSOP Club (Very Strong Old People) meets on Thursday mornings.

Care provided is welfare-level residential support: shelter, meals, basic daily assistance, access to medical attention when needed, and pastoral and emotional care. The home also assists with family reunification and funeral arrangements where required. This is not a nursing facility — there is no rehabilitation programme, dementia unit, or intensive medical capability, and the very small size means admission is selective and a waiting list likely exists.

Rumah Charis suits elderly individuals who are Christian or would welcome a Christian community setting, who are relatively mobile and independent, and whose families need welfare-level support rather than clinical nursing. Admission tends to prioritise those from lower-income backgrounds given the charity model. Call 03-7781 5977 to speak with the Operations Manager about the admission process, current availability, and whether any contribution is expected.

title: Seavoy Nursing Home (Taman Setapak Indah)
editorial_summary:
Seavoy Nursing Home's Setapak Indah branch sits on Jalan 2A/6 in a residential pocket of Setapak, and is the original site of a small chain founded in 1989 by Datin Dr Mangalaveni Karthigasu, a consultant physician who still leads the operator. The home is registered with the Ministry of Health and the Welfare Department (JKM), which is the baseline most families should look for. It is the older of two Seavoy houses in northern Kuala Lumpur, the second being a larger branch in Desa Melawati a few minutes' drive away.

The Setapak house takes both ambulant elderly residents and bed-bound patients, and runs day care alongside long and short stays. Stroke recovery and post-operative care are a stated focus, with physiotherapy provided in-house and a doctor on the team rather than only on call. Mandarin-speaking staff are available, which matters for Chinese-speaking families who want their parent to be understood at the bedside. Listed pricing for the chain starts around RM2,600 a month for shared 6-to-8-bed wards.

For families, Seavoy Setapak suits a parent who needs hands-on nursing at a mid-market price rather than a hotel-style suite. Worth confirming before you sign: how many residents share a room at the price you are quoted, the actual nurse-to-resident ratio overnight, whether the doctor visit is weekly or only on referral, and current bed availability since Seavoy is a recognisable name in KL and rooms can fill. Ask to see the licence certificate on the wall when you visit.

title: Seavoy Nursing Home (Desa Melawati)
longitude: Seavoy Nursing Home (Desa Melawati) is a care home located in Selangor. The home accommodates up to 140 residents.
editorial_summary:
The Desa Melawati branch of Seavoy Nursing Home occupies two linked shophouse units at No. 18 & 20, Jalan 5/4C, in the older residential part of Melawati at the foot of the Klang Gates quartz ridge. It is the larger of the two Seavoy homes and the one most visitors find first online. The chain was founded in 1989 by Datin Dr Mangalaveni Karthigasu, who runs it as both founder and consultant physician, and operates under Ministry of Health and JKM registration.

The team here includes physicians, registered nurses and physiotherapists, with Mandarin-speaking caregivers on shift. Care covers the full residential range a family in KL typically asks about: round-the-clock supervision for healthy seniors, monitoring of bedridden residents, post-operative recovery and stroke physiotherapy. Day care is offered for families who want a daytime arrangement before committing to a full move. Published rates start from about RM2,600 a month for a 6-to-8-bed shared ward and from around RM4,900 for a single room at this branch.

This is a practical, mid-market home rather than a premium one — it has been operating for over thirty years from converted shoplots, not a purpose-built facility. Reasonable questions on a visit: how many beds are in each room at the price you are being quoted, what the night-shift nurse number is, whether the doctor sees residents on a fixed schedule, and what the policy is when a resident's needs escalate (oxygen, PEG feeding, dementia wandering). Ask to see the JKM and MOH certificates.

title: Siri Jayanti Metta Care Centre
editorial_summary:
Siri Jayanti Metta Care Centre — locally known as Metta Home — is a welfare-oriented old folks home run by Pertubuhan Kebajikan Siri Jayanti (Siri Jayanti Welfare Organisation), a Buddhist charity founded in 1991. The home is located at 2 Jalan Perusahaan Satu, in the Kawasan Perusahaan PKNS industrial estate near Setapak, Kuala Lumpur — though some records list it under a Selangor postcode given the estate's administrative boundary. The facility relocated from Ampang to its current site in 2004 and has around 20 beds, based on the organisation's own published figures. It operates entirely on public donations, with no commercial fee model.

Metta Home's primary mission is to shelter neglected and low-income elderly who have no family support. The organisation explicitly states it accepts residents regardless of race, creed, or religion, despite its Buddhist character — a practical inclusivity uncommon in faith-based homes. The SJWO runs broader welfare programmes (food aid, education support, charity events) that give the home an active volunteer and donor network, which translates into periodic external support — charity visits, food donations, and fundraising drives that supplement day-to-day operations. The care model is basic residential support: shelter, meals, and companionship rather than clinical nursing.

Families should understand that Metta Home is a charity home, not a licensed nursing facility in the clinical sense. With roughly 20 residents and donation-dependent funding, staffing and facilities are modest. It is best suited for seniors who are relatively mobile and need supported living rather than complex nursing care. Families considering this home should visit in person, ask about the current resident-to-caregiver ratio, and understand what happens if a resident's health declines and clinical nursing becomes necessary. Admission is likely means-tested and subject to availability — contact the SJWO sub-committee directly to enquire.

title: Sri Seronok Retirement Village
editorial_summary:
Sri Seronok Retirement Village is an unusual property in Malaysia: a true low-density retirement village rather than a nursing home. It sits on a two-acre site in Taman Sri Bahtera off Jalan Cheras and is owned by the Catholic Archdiocese of Kuala Lumpur. The project was conceived in the late 1980s by the late Archbishop Soter Fernandez and opened to residents in 1999, with about thirty single-storey independent-living units of roughly 650 sq ft, each containing a bedroom, kitchen and dining area.

Residents live in their own units and look after their own daily needs — there is no on-site nursing, no medical team and no rehabilitation programme. Eligibility is fifty-five years and above, and applicants must be physically and cognitively able to live independently. Monthly rent is in the region of RM750 to RM800 with no ownership rights, which is unusually affordable for the location. The village is run quietly, does not advertise, and admissions are largely by word of mouth through the Catholic community; there is a long waiting list, reportedly twenty to thirty people deep.

Who this suits: an active, independent older adult, often Catholic, who wants their own front door, low rent and a community of neighbours rather than ward-style care. Who it does not suit: anyone needing help with bathing, medication, mobility, dementia or feeding — they would have to move out. Ask about the queue length, what triggers a notice to leave when health declines, religious-affiliation expectations during admission, and what happens if a couple admits one spouse who later becomes dependent.

title: Tai Kuk Wah Si Buddist Society
editorial_summary:
Tai Kuk Wah Si Buddhist Society operates a small residential care home attached to its temple complex in Taman Pertama, Cheras — a neighbourhood that straddles the Selangor-KL border along the Cheras corridor. The society is primarily known as a Buddhist religious organisation, and its elder care arm appears to be a welfare extension of that mission rather than a commercial nursing facility. With 20 listed beds and a phone number as the only public contact, the home is modest in scale and appears to cater to elderly residents who are Buddhist or who have no other means of support.

Public information on this home is sparse. Google Maps reviewers (29 reviews, 4.6 stars) suggest the home is operational and well-regarded within its community, but no website, Facebook page, or regulatory filing was found in open sources. The care model appears to rely on volunteer support and in-kind donations consistent with Buddhist welfare home traditions in the Klang Valley — a pattern shared by similar society-run homes that use temple networks for volunteer doctor visits and general upkeep.

Families considering this home should visit in person before committing. Confirm whether it is registered with the Jabatan Kebajikan Masyarakat (JKM), what medical oversight is in place, and whether professional nursing staff or volunteers provide daily care. The home's Buddhist religious character is likely embedded in daily routines, so families of other faiths should establish comfort levels upfront. Verify visiting policies, food arrangements, and the practical pathway for hospital transfers, as these details are not publicly documented.

title: Persatuan Kebajikan Dan Social Kim Loo Ting
editorial_summary:
Persatuan Kebajikan Dan Social Kim Loo Ting — known in Chinese as 甘露亭 — is a Buddhist welfare association operating a residential home for elderly homeless individuals in the Taman Ayer Panas (Setapak) area of the Kuala Lumpur-Selangor fringe. The home follows a charity model: it primarily accepts elderly residents who have been discharged from government hospitals with no family support and no fixed address, making it distinct from conventional fee-paying nursing homes. Capacity is listed at 25 beds, with men and women housed in separate rooms within the same premises.

Volunteer doctors from Tung Shin Hospital conduct free medical consultations for residents every Sunday, providing basic clinical oversight. The home maintains high cleanliness standards, with able-bodied residents contributing to daily upkeep alongside a small number of paid helpers. The Facebook page (listed as the website in the data) reflects the organisation's charitable outreach. The facility has operated for many years and is affiliated with Buddhist welfare networks in the Klang Valley, lending it some community credibility despite its modest infrastructure.

This home serves a specific social welfare function and is not suited to families seeking fee-based private nursing care. If you are enquiring on behalf of a destitute elderly person, call the centre directly at 03-40232350 to discuss admission criteria. Confirm the current JKM registration status, the frequency and scope of the volunteer doctor programme, and whether any nursing-level care (not just caregiver support) is available on-site. Families of paying residents should clarify what ongoing medical management looks like for complex conditions.

title: KLC Senior Care Center
longitude: KLC Senior Care Center is a care home located in Selangor. The home accommodates up to 50 residents.
editorial_summary:
KLC Senior Care is the Kuala Lumpur city-centre branch of an operator called The Senior Care, which runs a separate flagship in Petaling Jaya. The KL site positions itself in the upper end of the market and offers assisted living, short-term stays, day care and rehabilitation. Reachable on 03-2181 7399, it is one of a small group of KL homes designed to feel more like residences than wards, with a yoga area, physiotherapy room, garden and swimming pool listed among the amenities.

Clinical cover comes from a doctor team that does weekly resident check-ups, with the operator stating medical staff are reachable on call around the clock. Nurses and caregivers are on site twenty-four hours, and services include medication management, hospital follow-ups, physiotherapy, nutritional planning and structured activities. The home accepts residents needing help with activities of daily living rather than only ambulant seniors, but it is not pitched as a heavy-medical facility for ventilated or end-stage patients.

For families this is an option when the priority is environment and lifestyle rather than the lowest sticker price — fees at this category typically run several thousand ringgit a month, but KLC does not publish rates publicly, so ask for a written quote that breaks out room, care level, diapers, doctor visits and physio. Other questions worth asking: actual nurse-to-resident ratio at night, whether the swimming pool is shared with anyone else, how dementia wandering is handled, and what triggers a fee step-up if your parent becomes more dependent.

title: LYC Senior Living Care Center
longitude: LYC Senior Living Care Center is a care home located in Selangor. The home accommodates up to 40 residents.
editorial_summary:
LYC Senior Living sits at 12 Jalan Gallagher in Taman Duta, a quiet old-KL residential street near the diplomatic quarter. The operator is LYC Senior Living Care Centre Sdn Bhd, a subsidiary of LYC Healthcare Bhd — a healthcare group listed on Bursa Malaysia. Bursa listing is an ownership-transparency fact: it means the parent files audited financials and is subject to listing-rule disclosures. It does not, on its own, mean the eldercare subsidiary's clinical practice is independently audited, and families should treat the listed-company status as a governance signal rather than a clinical-quality one. The home is described as a 35-bed elderly rehabilitation centre, set in a colonial-style house with a garden.

The operator describes the clinical model as doctor-led, advertising care delivered by qualified medical doctors with on-site or on-call cover, board-certified nurses, and in-house physiotherapists. These are operator claims — the precise meaning of "on-site or on-call" matters a great deal in practice and should be pinned down. Amenities include a physiotherapy area, a yoga room, a TV lounge, garden space and a swimming pool. The 35-bed cap is small enough that residents are not anonymous, and the rehabilitation framing means the home is comfortable taking post-stroke, post-surgery and frailty cases rather than only ambulant retirees. In 2023 LYC Senior Living signed a service and collaboration agreement with Jasper Life Sdn Bhd, broadening its operator network.

This is a premium option and pricing is not published — expect to be quoted in the higher KL band and ask for a written breakdown of room, nursing level, doctor visits, diapers, physio sessions and any add-ons. Other practical questions: who the named attending doctor is and how often they actually round (versus on-call), the specific night-shift RN number, the dementia management policy, whether antipsychotic use is openly discussed, and whether respite stays can convert to permanent placement when a bed opens. Also ask whether the listed-parent governance translates to written care plans and audited internal records at the home itself — if it does not, the Bursa-listing label adds little to the daily care your relative will receive.

title: Pusat Jagaan En Yuan (Cheras)
editorial_summary:
Pusat Jagaan En Yuan is located at 5, Jalan 28, Kampung Cheras Baru, in the Cheras corridor on the Selangor-KL border. The sheet records a phone number of 03-4288 3001 and lists total beds at 50 — one of the few facilities in this batch with a disclosed capacity figure — making it a medium-sized home by Klang Valley standards. The area sits close to the Cheras-Kajang Expressway, giving it reasonable access from both Cheras and the inner Klang Valley. The facility holds a 4.2 Google rating across 20 reviews.

Online information for En Yuan is sparse. Third-party directory listings confirm the Kampung Cheras Baru address and categorise it as an assisted living and nursing home operation. The name 'En Yuan' (恩源) suggests Chinese-language management, which may be relevant for Mandarin-speaking families. The 50-bed capacity indicates a purpose-built or substantially converted facility rather than a single terrace house operation.

Because no website or Facebook presence was found for En Yuan, families should plan an in-person visit to assess care quality directly. Key questions to ask: what care types are accepted (independent, semi-dependent, fully bed-bound), whether a registered nurse is present overnight, what the monthly fee structure looks like, and whether the facility holds a current JKM licence. The 4.2 rating is decent but below average for well-regarded Klang Valley homes — reading recent individual reviews via Google Maps before visiting is worthwhile.

title: PJ Mentalink Care/ PJ Old Folks
editorial_summary:
PJ Mentalink Care, also operating under the names Pusat Jagaan Mentalink and Roy Comfort Care Centre, is a small Petaling Jaya group with an unusual scope: it began in 1998 as a private psychiatric residence and has expanded to run multiple homes in the PJ area, including an elderly arm. The flagship old-folks home is a 20-bed house in Section 8 PJ, minutes from University Hospital (PPUM), at 109 Jalan Templer. The group reports running eight homes in total across PJ.

Because the operator's clinical roots are in mental health, it accepts a broader range of cases than a typical eldercare home — Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, dementia with behavioural symptoms, and patients with co-existing psychiatric illness alongside more conventional bedridden, diabetic and post-stroke residents. Services include medication administration, vital sign monitoring, wound care, physiotherapy and palliative support. The team works in English, Malay and Mandarin, and the homes use a seven-day pre-admission isolation protocol for new arrivals.

For families, this is a place to consider when an elderly parent has a psychiatric or behavioural component that other homes have declined — that is a real market gap, but the clinical depth claimed here is not third-party verified and the safety bar should be set higher than usual. It is not a premium or high-design facility; expect a residential-house setting rather than a purpose-built centre. Confirm which of the eight houses your parent would actually live in, the staff-to-resident ratio at that specific house overnight, and JKM licence status for the chosen branch (each house should be licensed in its own right, not under a single group cover). For residents with active psychiatric symptoms specifically: ask who the prescribing psychiatrist is — not just the visiting one — and how prescription reviews happen, and ask to see the medication-administration record (MAR) for a current resident before placement so you can see how psychotropics are charted, given and reviewed in practice.

title: Pusat Jagaan Rumah Warga Tua Cahaya
editorial_summary:
Pusat Jagaan Rumah Warga Tua Cahaya is a registered care home situated at No. 2, Jalan Merpati 8, Puchong Jaya, 47100 Puchong, Selangor. Directory sources indicate it has been in operation since 2010 and is classified as an associations-type care provider rather than a corporate nursing home chain. The Google listing attributes a 4.5-star rating from 15 reviews and lists a total bed count of 76, which would make it a mid-sized facility relative to the Puchong neighbourhood home market. The listed phone number is 03-8071 0232.

The facility is described in public directories as offering day-care on a daily basis and full residential care on a monthly arrangement. With 76 beds on record, the home likely accommodates a mix of dependency levels. Specific nursing capabilities, medical visit frequency, and clinical services are best confirmed directly with the home. The 'associations' classification suggests the home may carry a welfare or community-care mandate alongside its residential operation.

Families considering this home should verify the 76-bed figure and current occupancy directly, and ask what proportion of residents are ambulant versus bed-bound. An in-person visit is the most reliable way to assess staffing ratios, hygiene standards, and the quality of the outdoor or communal areas. Useful questions to bring: whether trained nurses are on duty overnight, what the visiting-doctor frequency is, and the current JKM (Social Welfare Department) licence number.

title: Rumah Victory Elderly Home
editorial_summary:
Rumah Victory is a non-profit Christian welfare organisation founded in 1988 by the late Mr Philip Mok Sew Kwong. It started as a drug rehabilitation centre and has grown into three arms — drug rehabilitation, a children and youth home, and an elderly home. The elderly arm was added in 2005 and operates from 2 Jalan 8, Taman Bukit Kuchai, off the Puchong main road. The organisation reports having cared for over 300 elderly residents since inception and currently houses around 57.

This is a charity-model home, not a fee-paying private nursing home. It exists to take in elderly people who are abandoned, destitute or without family support, and is funded largely by donations — corporate CSR partners include groups such as RCE Capital and MOG Eyewear, who run hikes and outreach events for the home. Staffing is residential-care rather than medical; the home is not pitched as a place for complex nursing needs, palliative care or dementia behavioural management, and the operator does not publish a JKM licence number, doctor list or nurse ratio.

For a family, Rumah Victory is the right call when a relative has no other safety net rather than when you are choosing between paid nursing homes — and even then, places are not guaranteed and a referral path matters. Worth confirming: current intake criteria and the social-work documentation needed, whether the home can manage a parent's specific medical needs (diabetes insulin, wound care, dementia), the visiting doctor arrangement, religious expectations during stay, and the JKM registration status.

title: Vivekananda Shelter
editorial_summary:
Vivekananda Shelter is a small care home located in PJS 4, an older residential precinct within Petaling Jaya in Selangor. The name Vivekananda carries strong associations with the Indian Hindu tradition — Swami Vivekananda is a revered figure across the South Asian community in Malaysia — and the home likely serves primarily the Tamil-speaking Indian community, possibly with a Hindu cultural or religious orientation to daily life and dietary practice. With only 7 beds listed, this is one of the smallest homes in the directory, functioning as a genuinely intimate, family-scale setting rather than a formal institution.

Petaling Jaya has a well-developed private healthcare network nearby, including Assunta Hospital and several specialist clinics, which is a practical advantage for residents needing periodic medical attention. However, the home keeps a low online profile: no website or Facebook page has been found, and specific details on staffing, care types, pricing, and JKM registration status are not publicly available. Contact is via the landline 03-77818012. The very small size means the home's quality will reflect closely the character and commitment of the operator in charge, making a personal visit especially important.

When calling, ask about the languages spoken by staff — Tamil fluency is likely relevant for some prospective residents — as well as whether the home accepts bedridden or post-stroke residents at this small scale, what the overnight arrangement looks like with seven beds, and the current JKM licence status. Ask also about dietary practices, religious or cultural activities offered, and whether the home has experience managing chronic conditions such as diabetes or dementia. Given the limited capacity, expect that availability is tightly managed and that waiting may apply.

title: Attia Nursing Care Centre
longitude: Attia Nursing Care Centre is a care home located in Selangor. The home accommodates up to 20 residents.
editorial_summary:
Attia Nursing Care Centre — reached on the Selangor data at 03-62425786, which corresponds to the group's Taman Wahyu branch at 62, Jalan Sibu 13, Taman Wahyu Jalan Ipoh, Kuala Lumpur — is part of Attia Care Centres, a multi-branch operator that has been running nursing homes across the Klang Valley and Ipoh since 2011. The group operates seven locations in total, including branches in Subang Jaya, Petaling Jaya (SS19 and Seksyen 17), SS2, Bukit Damansara, Taman Wahyu, Klang, and Ipoh. This branch sits in Taman Wahyu off Jalan Ipoh, a well-connected corridor with convenient access to hospitals and specialist clinics in the northern KL arc. The operation is a full-time residential nursing home, not a day-care or drop-in facility.

The Taman Wahyu branch has a stated capacity of 22 beds. Across the Attia network, verified capabilities include 24-hour nursing support, physiotherapy and rehabilitation for post-stroke, coma, cancer, head injury, orthopaedic, and amputation cases, speech therapy, wound care, medication management, and palliative or hospice care. Rooms include a nurse-call system and the facility is designed for residents with significant nursing dependency. The group's person-centred care model emphasises individual care plans and round-the-clock staff availability. Home-cooked meals and structured activity programmes are also referenced across the network's marketing material.

Because Attia operates a network, families should confirm that the specific Taman Wahyu branch — rather than a sister branch — has the right bed availability and clinical capability for their relative's needs. Ask about nurse-to-resident ratios on both day and night shifts, which languages the care team speaks, and whether the branch holds its own individual JKM registration or operates under a group licence. Confirm current pricing for the level of care required, as the Taman Wahyu branch does not publish a fixed tariff. If the home is full, ask whether another Attia branch within reach has availability.

title: Pusat Jagaan Selangor King George V Silver Jubilee Fund
editorial_summary:
Selangor King George V Silver Jubilee Fund (SKGV) is one of Malaysia's oldest charitable care homes, established in 1937 in Pudu, Kuala Lumpur, to shelter destitute Chinese women who had migrated from China in the 1930s and found themselves sleeping on five-foot-ways. More than 85 years on, it remains rooted in the same mission: providing a home for elderly women aged 65 and above who are homeless, have no surviving dependents, and are broadly capable of self-care. The facility, registered with social welfare authorities under W/PJB WT 001/2017, sits at 3052 Jalan Jubilee, Pudu, and is run with a lean staff supplemented heavily by public donations and volunteer groups.

SKGV accommodates around 60 residents according to Google listing data. Care sits at the independent-to-low-dependency end of the spectrum — residents can largely look after themselves, with the home providing meals, shelter, and a community. Documented activities include vegetable gardening, daily Qigong exercise sessions, and regular visits from volunteer groups who conduct free health screenings and basic medical check-ups. Operational funding is donation-driven, covering groceries, medical aids, and consumables. The facility received a visit from the Prime Minister in February 2022, indicating some degree of public recognition.

Families looking at SKGV need to understand this is fundamentally a charitable refuge for women without family support — it is unlikely to accept residents who have family networks or who require nursing-level medical care, catheter management, dementia supervision, or specialist intervention. No private or semi-private pricing structure has been published. The contact person on record is Ms Fannie Poon (010-280 3380; office: 03-9223 4891). Ask directly about admission criteria, what medical care is available on-site versus referred out, and whether places are available given the modest published capacity.

title: Berniece Care Service Centre
editorial_summary:
Berniece Care Centre has been operating since 1998 and moved to its current site at 18B Jalan Chempedak, off Jalan Kovil Hilir in Sentul, in 2000. It is a small, long-running Christian-run home that takes both live-in residents and day care clients, and is registered with the Ministry of Health and Welfare. The operator describes the home as Christian in identity but open to residents of any faith.

It is a small house with around twenty residents at any time, a mixed-gender population aged roughly sixty to ninety. Care covers the bread-and-butter residential elderly profile: routine personal care, post-hospital recovery, and stroke patients needing physiotherapy, which can be arranged. Meals are home-cooked rather than catered, which residents and families often value. The operator does not advertise a doctor on staff, so medical input is likely on referral rather than embedded — expected for a home of this size.

For families, Berniece sits in the small, family-style end of the market — closer to a household than an institution. That suits a parent who would feel lost in a large facility but not one needing tight nursing oversight. Practical questions before signing: who handles medication rounds and how that is recorded, the night-shift staff number for twenty residents, what happens if a resident's needs escalate (PEG feeding, oxygen, dementia wandering), how visiting and community involvement work given the Christian setting, and whether the Sentul location's parking and access work for your family's visiting routine.

title: Green Cottage Retirement and Nursing Home
Columbia Asia Extended Care Hospital
editorial_summary:
Columbia Asia Extended Care Hospital in Section 17, Shah Alam, is the oldest facility in the Columbia Asia private hospital group — opened in 1998 and reportedly the group's first Malaysian property. Unlike the group's other acute hospitals, this facility is designed from the ground up for extended stays: long-term nursing care, post-hospitalisation step-down, and inpatient rehabilitation, rather than acute surgery or emergency medicine. It is licensed and operates as a private hospital with resident and on-call doctors.

The hospital has 65 beds and provides 24-hour nursing care alongside physiotherapy, occupational therapy, and speech therapy. Published rehabilitation packages give an indication of pricing: a 10-day Neuro Recovery Package is listed at RM 7,500 and a 30-day version at RM 30,000; an Orthopaedic Recovery Package starts at RM 3,000. Long-term nursing placements for conditions such as cancer or post-stroke dependence are also offered. Doctor house-call services extend beyond the hospital walls. The facility has visiting hours (11:30am–2:30pm and 4:30pm–8:30pm), which suggests a reasonably structured clinical environment.

This is a strong option for families who need a medically supervised step-down placement after an acute hospital stay — particularly for neurological recovery or complex nursing cases where a standard residential home may lack clinical depth. The pricing tier is higher than most community nursing homes; clarify whether your family member's needs fit the rehabilitation pathway or long-term nursing track, as the cost structures differ. Insurance coverage through major providers is worth confirming before admission.

title: Pusat Jagaan Ebenezer Home
editorial_summary:
Pusat Jagaan Ebenezer Home is a care home located in Mutiara Bukit Kemuning, within the Seksyen 25 area of Shah Alam, Selangor — specifically at Lot 1273, Batu 8, Jalan Bukit Kemuning. The biblical name Ebenezer (meaning stone of help) indicates a Christian-founded home, and the facility appears to serve broadly, likely drawing residents from the Chinese-Malaysian and other communities in the Shah Alam and Klang corridor. With 60 beds listed, this is one of the larger homes in this batch, operating at a scale that suggests a more structured care environment with dedicated staffing rather than the boutique, family-house model.

A Facebook page exists for the home under the name Pusat Jagaan Ebenezer Home — Shah Alam, Selangor, and the home appears in the MDAC directory, confirming it has some registration or listing presence with care-sector bodies. However, a standalone website has not been found, and detailed information about care types, staffing ratios, nursing qualifications, pricing, and specific programme offerings is not publicly available. Contact numbers in the source data are 016-355 2248 and 011-2621 2084. The nearby Kota Kemuning and Bukit Kemuning area has growing healthcare infrastructure, and Shah Alam's government and private hospital network provides a reasonable medical safety net.

Given the 60-bed scale, this home likely has a more formal internal structure than a small house-style operator. Key questions to raise on first contact include: what care dependency levels the home accepts (ambulant, assisted, bedridden, dementia), what the nursing-to-carer staff composition looks like, whether there is a JKM licence in good standing, and how religious programming is integrated into daily life. Ask about meal arrangements, visiting hours, and whether Chinese-language or Tamil-language staff are on the team. A visit is especially worthwhile at this size to assess common areas, room configurations, and the overall environment.

title: Bethel Retirement Home PLT
editorial_summary:
Bethel Retirement Home PLT is listed in Kawasan 6, Shah Alam, registered as a Limited Liability Partnership (PLT). The 'Bethel' name carries a Christian connotation, which often signals a faith-aligned operating ethos, though this is best confirmed directly. The home is contactable on 03-3381 6742.

The home keeps a low online profile and does not maintain a published website or active social media page, so most service detail is best gathered on a call. Bed count, the scope of medical care, religious orientation, and JKM registration are best confirmed directly with the operator. Kawasan 6 is a mixed residential-industrial neighbourhood in central Shah Alam, supporting a number of smaller community-scale care homes.

Families considering Bethel should call ahead to establish current admission availability and what level of care the home is set up to deliver. Useful questions: bed count, registered-nurse cover, doctor-visit frequency, dependency levels accepted, religious or dietary considerations, monthly fees, and the JKM licence number. A short visit during a meal or activity time will give a clearer sense of the home's daily rhythm and whether it suits a particular resident.

title: Pusat Jagaan Chik Sin Thong Klang Dan Pantai Selangor
editorial_summary:
Pusat Jagaan Chik Sin Thong is a charitable old folks home in Klang, run by The Association of Klang and Coast Chik Sin Thong Old Folks Home Selangor. The main site sits on Persiaran Tengku Ampuan Rahimah at Batu 1 1/4, on the edge of Taman Sri Andalas. The association is a long-standing Chinese-community charity and the home relies heavily on public donations, with regular appeals for adult diapers, toiletries, fresh produce and other consumables published on its website.

Residents are admitted via a written application form available from the office, and the home provides custodial residential care for older adults who cannot be looked after at home, including those with limited financial means. Operations are bilingual Chinese and English on paper but Mandarin and Hokkien dominate day-to-day life. Specific bed counts, nurse coverage and clinical capabilities are not published, although an active intake process and the standing donation list suggest a steady population of around 30 residents in line with the directory entry.

This suits families seeking a charitable home for a Mandarin- or Hokkien-speaking elder with low to moderate care needs, rather than complex medical cases. Because the home does not advertise nursing accreditation or doctor coverage, families should ask in detail about overnight staffing, how medical emergencies are handled, whether residents needing tube feeding or oxygen are accepted, eligibility criteria for charitable placement, and any expected family contribution.

title: Pusat Jagaan Persatuan Kebajikan Rumah Jagaan VCU Selangor Dan Wilayah Persekutuan Kuala Lumpur
editorial_summary:
Persatuan Kebajikan Rumah Jagaan VCFU is a registered welfare-society nursing home in Klang, operating from No. 65, Lorong Mohd Tahir 6B, Taman Saujana. It is registered with Jabatan Kebajikan Masyarakat under licence B/PJB WT 015/2017 and runs as a non-profit welfare association rather than a private commercial chain. The home is reachable on 03-3372 5888 and 010-213 0256, and on email at vc4uhomeklang@gmail.com. Note that the website occasionally cited for this facility (srieden.org) belongs to an unrelated children's special-needs organisation and should not be used as the source of truth for VCFU.

As a welfare-society home in suburban Klang, VCFU is set up for general residential elder care — long-term stay for older adults whose families cannot provide round-the-clock supervision at home. Detailed clinical capabilities, staffing ratios, and the precise mix of mobile, semi-dependent, and bedridden residents are not published on any verified channel, so families should confirm directly whether the home accepts cases requiring tube feeding, oxygen, wound dressing, or stroke rehabilitation. Pricing is not publicly listed and is set on assessment.

For families considering VCFU, the practical steps are to phone ahead, request the JKM operating licence and a written fee breakdown, and visit in person before committing. Ask specifically about nurse-to-resident ratio overnight, how medication rounds are documented, and what happens when a resident becomes acutely unwell — which hospital they are sent to, who escorts, and whether the family is contacted before transfer. A welfare-society structure can mean lower fees than commercial homes, but it also means scrutinising staffing and medical backup is more important, not less.

title: Rumah Kebajikan Chong Keng Seong
longitude: Rumah Kebajikan Chong Keng Seong is a care home located in Selangor. The home accommodates up to 32 residents.
editorial_summary:
Rumah Kebajikan Chong Keng Seong — known in shorthand as Rumah Orang Tua CKS — is a welfare home for the elderly located at No. 80A, Jalan Bukit Mertajam, off Jalan Kapar, in the Klang district of Selangor. The name commemorates the home's founder, and the welfare home (rumah kebajikan) classification indicates a charity or non-profit model of operation rather than a private commercial home, which typically means residents may include those who have limited family support or financial means. Klang's location provides reasonable proximity to Hospital Tengku Ampuan Rahimah and a broad network of local clinics. With 32 beds listed, the home operates at a community-welfare scale.

The home keeps a very low digital profile. No website or social media presence has been found, and the directional contact is through En Cheong Choong Chin at 03-3344 1028. The home's history appears in older community directories of Klang Valley welfare homes, suggesting it has operated for a considerable period. As a welfare home, the emphasis is likely on social support and basic residential care for ambulant or semi-independent elderly rather than high-acuity nursing, but this is unverified and should be confirmed directly.

Families considering this home for a relative should understand the welfare-home model clearly: ask whether the home has a current JKM registration and what its admissions criteria are — specifically whether it prioritises low-income residents or accepts self-paying residents at a standard rate. Confirm what level of care dependency is accepted, what overnight staffing looks like across 32 residents, and whether there is nursing or medical staff on the team. Ask about languages spoken by carers, dietary arrangements, and visiting policy. A site visit is the most reliable way to assess the physical condition of the facility and the day-to-day quality of care.

title: Pusat Jagaan Beverly Care Home
longitude: Pusat Jagaan Beverly Care Home is a care home located in Selangor. The home accommodates up to 23 residents.
editorial_summary:
Pusat Jagaan Beverly Care Home is a care home in Selangor, with the phone number 03-87370076 pointing to the Kajang or Sungai Long area. Cross-referencing available data, Beverly Care Home is associated with an address at Jalan Ikhlas, Taman Ikhlas, Sungai Long, Kajang — operated by Madam Foo and positioned as a home-like setting for elderly residents. Sungai Long sits between Kajang town and Cheras, giving residents reasonable access to the Kajang hospital network and the healthcare corridor along the Cheras–Kajang highway. With 23 beds listed, the home occupies a mid-range scale that sits between a boutique house-style home and a larger institutional facility.

Pricing information found via a third-party source suggests a tiered room structure: dormitory-style four-bed rooms at approximately RM 2,000–2,300 per month, three-bed rooms at around RM 2,500, and two-bed rooms at approximately RM 2,700 — figures that are unverified and may have changed. A dedicated website at beverlycarehome.com exists but was inaccessible at time of research, so care type details, staffing information, and programme descriptions could not be confirmed from first-party sources. The home keeps a relatively low online profile outside its own website.

On a first call to 03-87370076, ask about the current room and pricing breakdown, what levels of care dependency the home accepts, and whether there is a trained nurse on site or on call. Confirm the JKM registration status, languages spoken by the care team, and whether the home has experience with residents who have dementia, stroke history, or are fully bedridden. Visiting hours policy and meal arrangements are also worth clarifying early. A site visit is recommended to see the actual room configurations and assess the environment before making a decision.

title: Pusat Jagaan Rumah Amal Murni Kajang
editorial_summary:
Persatuan Rumah Amal Murni Kajang is a registered non-profit charity home in Kajang, established on 4 April 2005 and managed by a volunteer-led committee since September 2006. The home is at 58 and 59 Jalan Bukit, Taman Bukit, Kajang, and is funded entirely by public donations and in-kind community support rather than government grants or fee-paying residents. Despite the name, the home is not Islamic-only — its blog and donor lists indicate it is a multi-faith Chinese-led charity (Persatuan Rumah Amal Murni Kajang 慈怀乐龄残障之家).

The home runs 24 hours a day and houses around 30 residents drawn from three groups: senior citizens with no family support, homeless adults, and people with mental or physical disabilities. Care is provided free of charge to carefully selected residents, and the organisation also distributes monthly supplies to struggling families in the surrounding area and donates excess goods to other shelters. Day-to-day care is custodial — meals, hygiene, supervision and basic assistance — rather than medical nursing.

This is not a clinical nursing facility, so it suits dependent adults whose primary need is shelter, food, hygiene and supervision, not those needing tube feeding, oxygen, or active medical management. Admission is selective and not guaranteed. Families enquiring should ask about the application criteria, what level of mobility or behavioural difficulty the home will accept, whether any nursing or medical staff visit, and how the home handles hospital transfers when residents fall acutely unwell.

title: Pusat Jagaan Al Fikrah Malaysia
editorial_summary:
Pusat Jagaan Al-Fikrah Malaysia is a welfare home — not a fee-based nursing home — located at Lot 683, Jalan Sekolah, Kampung Sungai Sekamat, off Jalan Cheras, Kajang, Selangor. It was established in August 2013 and is run as an NGO under a committee chaired by Haji Mohd Azmi Bin Mohd Zain. The home is Islamic in character and serves a mix of populations: elderly residents without family support, chronically ill individuals, converts to Islam, people with disabilities, and those discharged from hospital with nowhere to go. The current capacity is approximately 70 residents.

The centre accepts both men and women. Basic medical services are provided through outreach — UKM Medical Centre's community health unit and other university faculties have conducted health screening visits, suggesting some external medical support, though the home does not appear to operate with resident nurses or doctors on permanent staff. Residents participate in vocational activities including gardening and small-scale animal husbandry. Funding comes from government grants, organisational donations, and public contributions via Islamic bank accounts (Maybank Islamic and Bank Muamalat).

This home serves a different function from a private nursing home. It is appropriate for elderly Muslims who have no family support network and no means to afford private care — or for families who need a placement of last resort. It is not designed for high-dependency nursing, dementia specialisation, or complex clinical care. Families seeking a placement here should contact the committee directly to understand current capacity, admission criteria, and what level of ongoing family involvement is expected or possible.

title: Mintygreen Nursing Home (ElderCare Center)
editorial_summary:
Mintygreen Nursing Home in Sungai Long is the headquarters branch of the Mintygreen Healthcare Group, a Malaysian eldercare chain that has grown to around seven branches across the Klang Valley and Penang. The Sungai Long house occupies a six-shoplot block at Landmark Residences 2 in Bandar Sungai Long, Kajang, and serves the southern Klang Valley catchment from Balakong and Seri Kembangan through to Kajang town. It is one of the chain's better-reviewed sites, with a 4.6-star Google rating across more than a hundred reviews.

Clinically, Mintygreen Sungai Long handles the full residential range from semi-dependent residents to fully bedridden patients. The service list includes day and overnight care, hospice and palliative care, wound management, feeding tube and catheter care, medication administration, and bi-weekly doctor visits, with traditional Chinese medicine layered in — acupuncture, tui na massage and TCM physician consults — which is a distinguishing feature of the Mintygreen model. Rooms have call bells, the bathrooms are wheelchair-accessible and grab bars run along walkways. Staff include nurses, physiotherapists, TCM physicians, mental-health practitioners and counsellors.

For families this is a mid-to-upper market option pitched at Chinese-speaking households comfortable with TCM alongside Western medicine. Visiting hours are 10am to 6pm and the contact at this branch is Ms Ru Jun on 018-987 3862. Questions to ask before committing: published room rates and what is included, the actual nurse-to-resident ratio overnight at this specific shoplot block, the doctor's name and visit cadence, dementia management protocol, JKM licence number for the Sungai Long site, and whether TCM treatments are extra-charge or bundled.

title: Kajang Nursing Home
longitude: Kajang Nursing Home is a care home located in Selangor. The home accommodates up to 10 residents.
editorial_summary:
Kajang Nursing Home is a small residential care facility in the Kajang district of Selangor, listed with a capacity of 10 beds. Its compact size places it among the more intimate homes in the area, where the Kajang corridor has a reasonable spread of registered care options. The facility keeps a low online profile — no website or social-media presence has been identified — so the best way to understand its day-to-day character is through a direct call or a site visit.

With 10 beds the home operates on a scale that typically suits families seeking a quieter, less institutional environment. Specific care capabilities — whether the home accepts bedridden, post-stroke, or dementia residents — are not documented in publicly available sources, and the range of nursing credentials on staff is similarly unconfirmed. The contact number on record is 03-8734 1596, which remains the primary point of inquiry for service details.

Because this is one of the smaller registered homes in Kajang, families should phone ahead to clarify the resident-to-carer ratio, the types of dependency the home can safely manage, and whether the facility holds a current Social Welfare Department (JKM) licence. Ask also about visiting hours and what happens if a resident needs hospital transfer — proximity to Kajang Hospital and KPJ Kajang Specialist, both a short drive away, is a practical plus worth factoring into the conversation.

title: Sam Ching Kong Old Folks Home, Hulu Langat
editorial_summary:
Sam Ching Kong Old Folks Home is a non-profit welfare home operated by the Sam Ching Kong Temple (三清宮道院与慈善福利會), a Taoist temple and charitable welfare society located in Kampung Baru Sungai Makau, Hulu Langat. The address is listed as Hulu Langat Batu 18, Sungai Makau, 43100 Hulu Langat, Selangor — a semi-rural location approximately 30 kilometres from central Kuala Lumpur. The home operates under the temple's charitable mission, offering a subsidised or donation-supported residential option for elderly individuals in need.

The facility's Google listing describes a clean and peaceful environment with friendly caretakers, suggesting a community-run home with a focus on basic residential care and companionship rather than clinical nursing. The data sheet records 50 beds, which would make it a mid-sized community welfare home by Selangor standards. As a temple-run facility in the Taoist tradition, it is likely to cater primarily to Buddhist and Taoist Chinese-speaking elderly, though this should be confirmed directly. No details are publicly available about nursing staffing levels, medical protocols, or dementia-specific programming.

Given its non-profit, donation-supported model, Sam Ching Kong is likely a more affordable option than private nursing homes — but families should ask directly about fees, if any, and whether government subsidies are available for eligible residents. Confirm whether registered nurses are on staff or whether care is primarily provided by lay caregivers, and clarify what medical emergencies protocol looks like given the semi-rural location. The nearest major hospital is likely Hospital Kajang or Hulu Langat District Hospital — ask about typical transfer times and the facility's relationship with local healthcare services.

title: Pusat Jagaan Orang Tua Apex Way
editorial_summary:
Pusat Jagaan Orang Tua Apex Way is a private residential elderly care home (registered category: W/T Berkediaman Swasta) at Jalan LP 4/5, Taman Lestari Perdana, Seri Kembangan — a well-connected suburb southeast of Kuala Lumpur. The facility can be reached on 03-89429993 and is listed with a reported capacity of 30 beds. It sits in a neighbourhood served by multiple bus routes (540, T543, T545, 541, SJ05) and is within the MRT network's catchment, making it reasonably accessible for visiting family members without a car.

The home keeps a low online profile, with no published website or Facebook page, so most service detail is best gathered on a call or visit. As a registered private residential home in the Seri Kembangan corridor, Apex Way is positioned to provide basic to moderate-dependency residential care. Specific clinical capabilities — registered-nurse coverage, doctor-visit frequency, wound-care, and dementia capacity — are best confirmed directly with the home.

Families should use an initial phone call to establish the current JKM operating-licence number, the level of medical supervision on-site, and how the home handles residents who develop higher-acuity needs over time. The Seri Kembangan location places it reasonably close to Columbia Asia Hospital Cheras and several GP clinics, which is a practical asset if emergencies arise. A site visit before committing is advisable for any smaller community-scale home.

title: Pusat Warga Mas Penyayang
editorial_summary:
Pusat Warga Mas Penyayang — operating under the name Kuan Ai Pusat Warga Mas Penyayang — is an elderly care service located at Jalan 3/1 in Taman Serdang Jaya, Seri Kembangan, within the southern Selangor corridor near the Serdang–Kajang axis. The facility holds 40 beds according to directory records, making it a mid-sized community-oriented home in a township that sits equidistant from Serdang Hospital and the Seri Kembangan commercial belt. The name Kuan Ai carries a Hokkien charitable resonance, suggesting a community welfare background rather than a commercial chain.

Publicly available information about this facility is sparse. It is listed as an elderly care service in business directories, with a recorded operating schedule of Monday to Friday, 8 am to 5 pm. That weekday-only schedule is worth noting: it may indicate a day-care-primary model, a skeleton live-in arrangement, or simply that administrative and visiting hours are restricted to weekdays. The Google Maps rating sits at 3.6 from eight reviews — a modest score that warrants investigation. No website beyond a Facebook page linked to the Kuan Ai name is publicly maintained, which limits independent verification of care scope.

Families considering this centre should call directly to clarify whether it offers 24-hour residential nursing care or primarily day-care placement, and whether live-in staff are present overnight. Given the 40-bed capacity and community-welfare framing, pricing may be more accessible than commercial chains — but ask for written confirmation. The 3.6 rating on a small sample is not alarming but does suggest requesting references from current residents' families. Proximity to Serdang Hospital (a government teaching hospital) is a practical plus for medical emergencies.

title: Wan Hing Care Center Sdn Bhd
editorial_summary:
Wan Hing Care Center is a Chinese-operated group of elderly care homes clustered in Seri Kembangan, Selangor, operating under the brand 温馨之家 ("Warm Home"). The listing in the Pusat Perdagangan Seri Kembangan area appears to correspond to Centre 4 (PSK), located at Jalan PSK 2, though the group runs at least three residential branches and a separate home-care arm in the same town. The homes position themselves as affordable, homelike facilities for seniors who need ongoing nursing support rather than a hospital setting.

Across the Wan Hing network, verified care conditions include stroke rehabilitation, dementia care, Parkinson's management, and post-fracture recovery. The PSK branch has approximately 26 beds in a mix of twin rooms with attached toilet, twin rooms with shared toilet, triple rooms, and dormitory-style accommodation. Monthly rates are reported to range from around RM 1,001 to RM 3,000 depending on room type and dependency level. Staff at Centre 3 have cited Columbia Asia Hospital Cheras — roughly 15 minutes away — as their nearest hospital reference.

Because Wan Hing operates multiple branches under the same brand, it is worth confirming which specific branch you are enquiring about and whether it holds a current JKM residential-care licence. Ask about nurse-to-resident ratios on overnight shifts, what constitutes "high dependency" for pricing purposes, and whether dementia residents are accommodated in a dedicated wing or mixed with the general population. Halal status and dietary options are also worth confirming given a mixed-community clientele.

title: Wan Hing Care Center (PSK Sdn Bhd)
longitude: Wan Hing Care Center (PSK Sdn Bhd) is a care home located in Selangor. The home accommodates up to 34 residents.
editorial_summary:
Wan Hing Care Center (PSK) Sdn Bhd is an established elderly care provider in the Seri Kembangan area of Selangor, operating under the Chinese name 温馨之家 (Warm Home). The group runs several branches across Seri Kembangan — including centres numbered 3 and 4 — with the PSK entity headquartered at Jalan PSK 2, Pusat Perdagangan Seri Kembangan, 43300 Seri Kembangan. The batch entry for this home lists 34 beds, consistent with a mid-sized community nursing home. The Wan Hing group has built its reputation on serving seniors who need short- or long-term nursing support in a Chinese-community setting.

The centre explicitly caters to residents with stroke, dementia, Parkinson's disease, and post-fracture rehabilitation needs, covering both short-stay convalescence and long-term residential care. Room configurations across the group's branches include twin rooms with attached toilet, twin rooms with shared toilet, triple rooms, and dormitory-style accommodation, reflecting a range of price points. Monthly fees across the Wan Hing network have been cited in the RM 1,000–3,000 range depending on room type and care level. Professional nursing care and a warm, family-like environment are central to the group's stated ethos.

Families considering this home should call 012-3110411 to confirm which branch has current availability — the PSK entity and the numbered branches share branding but operate at separate addresses. It is worth asking specifically about nursing staff qualifications, the dementia care approach, and whether the home can manage PEG feeding or oxygen therapy. Language is unlikely to be a barrier for Mandarin- and Cantonese-speaking families, as the home's Chinese-language Facebook presence suggests staff are comfortable in those languages; confirm Malay or English support if needed.

title: Pusat Jagaan Mahmudah Malaysia
editorial_summary:
Pusat Jagaan Mahmudah Malaysia is a registered care centre at Lot 459/5, Jalan 1 Off Jalan Bangi Lama, Kampung Sungai Purun, 43500 Semenyih, Selangor — note the directory metadata lists this only as 'Selangor', but the actual location is Semenyih in Hulu Langat district. The centre was established in 2016 under the Care Centres Act 1993 and is registered with Jabatan Kebajikan Masyarakat. It is operated by the welfare organisation Persatuan Kebajikan Mahmudah Malaysia rather than a private company, which gives it a charitable rather than commercial structure.

The home cares for residents aged 38 to 90 — a wider age range than a typical nursing home — and current census reported in public coverage is around 47 residents, despite directory listings citing a 100-bed capacity (the gap is worth verifying). The population skews towards vulnerable cases: residents abandoned by family, those whose families cannot afford private care, persons with disabilities, individuals needing ongoing medical attention, and bedbound patients with non-communicable diseases requiring routine review. The centre's main public channel is Facebook rather than a standalone website.

Pusat Jagaan Mahmudah suits the welfare end of the eldercare spectrum — it is a meaningful option for Muslim Malay families in the Semenyih/Bangi corridor with limited financial means, or for cases where commercial homes have declined a placement. Families enquiring should expect the model to differ from a private nursing home: ask about funding arrangements (donations, JKM subsidy, family contribution), staff qualifications, clinical equipment for bedbound care, and whether placements depend on case approval by the welfare organisation rather than direct booking.

title: Pusat Jagaan Dan Pendidikan Warga Emas Darul Insyirah
editorial_summary:
Pusat Jagaan Dan Pendidikan Warga Emas Darul Insyirah is a Shariah-compliant elderly care centre in Bandar Baru Bangi, Seksyen 3, Selangor, operating under the registered welfare body Pertubuhan Kebajikan Darul Insyirah. What distinguishes it from most private nursing homes in the Klang Valley is its dual identity as both a care facility and an Islamic education centre: the name explicitly includes pendidikan (education), and daily programming incorporates Quranic recitation classes, religious lectures, and structured acts of ibadah. It is designed for Muslim seniors who wish to spend their remaining years in a spiritually enriching, Shariah-conscious environment rather than a purely clinical one. The facility is listed with 20 beds and is contactable at 03-89209661.

Darul Insyirah offers care across three tiers — daily, weekly, and monthly residential stays — accommodating both ambulant seniors seeking companionship and structured spiritual activity, and those who require hands-on physical assistance due to illness or frailty. The centre has been featured in Malaysian media for its philosophy that a care home should strengthen, not replace, family responsibility, and the founders are vocal advocates for dignifying elderly Muslims in Malaysia. A larger "Smart Caring Home" complex — designed for approximately 150 residents and incorporating elderly-friendly architecture, prayer-friendly spaces, and green technology — has been publicly fundraised for expansion.

Monthly fees have been cited publicly at RM 1,200–1,800 depending on care level, which positions Darul Insyirah among the more affordable options in the Bangi corridor for families seeking an Islamic residential home. Prospective residents should call to confirm current bed availability, the level of nursing care provided for medically dependent residents, and whether the home accepts government subsidy or JKM referrals. Families should also ask about visiting arrangements, the qualification of nursing staff, and how the centre handles residents requiring hospital-level intervention.

title: Pusat Jagaan Rumah Nur Hasanah
editorial_summary:
Pusat Jagaan Nur Hasanah (PJNH) is a specialised transit home for displaced male patients in Beranang, southern Selangor — a distinctly different model from private nursing homes. Established in September 2006 and operated under the Care Centre Act 1993, the facility sits at Lot 842, Lorong Dato' Dagang Haji Tahir, Kampung Sesapan Kelubi, 43700 Beranang. It does not serve private-paying families in the conventional sense: admission is through hospital Medical Social Work Departments, and the population served is male patients without family support who have been discharged or are awaiting permanent placement by the Department of Social Welfare (JKM). Residents include the elderly, persons with disabilities, new Muslims (muallaf), and those otherwise classified as destitute. The listed bed capacity is 30, and since founding, more than 220 residents have passed through.

The care model centres on stabilisation and transitional support rather than long-term residency. Residents typically stay up to six months while social workers work to secure permanent placement at government homes such as Rumah Seri Kenangan or Rumah Ehsan. Programmes include counselling, spiritual and religious activities, recreation, and medical care with transport assistance. The facility is financially sustained through individual, corporate, and government donations, which are eligible for income tax deduction under Section 44(6) of the Income Tax Act — a sign of its formalised charitable status.

Families with a private-pay budget or a relative requiring standard nursing home placement will find PJNH outside the typical options they are comparing. This facility exists to address a specific welfare gap for patients with no family or financial resources. Those who are eligible — and whose loved one meets the criteria — must route applications through hospital social workers, not direct contact with the facility. Confirm the current referral process and available capacity with PJNH directly at 03-87668100, as transit demand from hospitals fluctuates.

title: Sincere Care Home
longitude: Sincere Care Home is a care home located in Selangor. The home accommodates up to 47 residents.
editorial_summary:
Sincere Care Home (also registered as Pertubuhan Ikhlas Malaysia) is a long-running elderly care home in Kajang, Selangor, that has been operating since 2002. Situated off Jalan Semenyih with a secondary address at Lot 18367, Jalan Kelapa, 43000 Kajang, the home is positioned within a short drive of Kajang Hospital and KPJ Kajang Specialist — a practical advantage for residents who need regular hospital appointments. With a listed capacity of 47 beds, it is one of the larger registered community homes in the Kajang area. The organisation has historically taken in residents who are elderly, disabled, abused, or abandoned, and whose families are unable to provide full-time medical and geriatric support.

Sincere Care Home's service scope covers nursing care and bedridden resident support, indoor and outdoor activities, exercise programmes, and transportation for hospital appointments. The home's Facebook page under the name Sincere Care Home | Kajang shows community engagement and accepts donations, consistent with its welfare-oriented origins. Three branches are referenced across Kajang, Seri Kembangan, and Dengkil under the broader Sincere Care network, suggesting accumulated operational experience across the Klang Valley's southern corridor. Visiting hours are listed as 08:00–17:00 daily.

Because the home's published digital presence is limited, families should call to clarify the specific nursing capabilities on site — particularly whether the home manages post-stroke, dementia, or palliative-stage residents — and what the staff-to-resident ratio looks like across shifts. Asking about the JKM licence status and current bed availability at the Kajang location (rather than the wider group) will help avoid any ambiguity. The primary contact listed publicly is Madam Danapakiyam at 014-3381609; the batch-sheet number 03-87341609 is an alternative line for the same facility.

title: Sri Sayang Welfare Home
editorial_summary:
Sri Sayang Welfare Home is a welfare-oriented residential facility for the aged and physically handicapped, established in 2003 at No. 27 Jalan Kelisa 4C/3, Taman Desa Kelisa, Batang Kali in Hulu Selangor — well outside the Klang Valley's urban core, roughly 60 km north of KL. This is a distinctly rural placement: Batang Kali / Ulu Yam Bharu sits at the foothills of Titiwangsa, and families considering this home should factor in travel time for regular visits. The facility holds registration number B/PJB P 002/2020 and lists an administrative contact number (03-6075 3475 / 03-6075 1554). Office hours appear to be weekday mornings only (8am–5pm).

Sri Sayang is evidenced as a Chinese community-associated home: a 2014 Wesak Day visit organised by corporate volunteers, and a 2018 Chinese New Year visit by Main Place Mall that included ang pao distribution and Tai Chi, both confirm a predominantly Chinese Buddhist resident population and cultural programming aligned with that community. The home accepts residents with physical handicaps in addition to elderly residents, suggesting a broader dependency profile than a pure nursing home. Reported capacity is 55 beds, which makes it one of the larger facilities in this batch despite its rural location.

Because the facility is far from tertiary medical facilities, families should explicitly ask about medical emergency protocols — the nearest hospital is likely Hospital Kuala Kubu Bharu, approximately 15 km away. Ask whether a nurse or doctor visits on a scheduled basis, how the home handles residents who develop acute conditions, and what the staffing ratio looks like on weekends when administrative staff are absent. The rural setting can offer a calm, low-stimulation environment that suits some elderly residents, but it requires strong family confidence in the home's clinical oversight.

title: Bellevue Residential Homecare (M) Sdn Bhd
editorial_summary:
Bellevue Residential Homecare (M) Sdn Bhd was established in 1992 and operates a 60-bed home at No. 9, Jalan 5/35, Petaling Jaya — in the Bukit Gasing pocket of Section 5. Three decades of continuous operation make Bellevue one of the older private retirement homes in the Klang Valley, and the operator publishes both a landline (03-7955 7726) and a mobile contact, with a Facebook presence used to document residents' activities and family events.

The home accepts retired, aged, handicapped and convalescent residents requiring 24-hour care — that is, both ambulatory residents and those needing more hands-on nursing support. Meals are provided in-house, and the activities calendar visible online includes Chinese New Year celebrations, excursions and visits from external community groups, suggesting an active rather than purely custodial setting. The website does not break out clinical capabilities (wound care, palliative, dementia track) in detail.

Bellevue is likely to suit families looking for a long-running, mid-sized PJ home with an established track record, rather than a clinical nursing facility. The 60-bed scale is modest enough to feel personal but large enough to sustain a real activities programme. Specific things to ask before placement: registered nurse coverage (especially overnight), whether a doctor visits routinely, monthly pricing by room type, halal/dietary options, and how the home handles dementia residents who become bedbound — none of which are spelled out on the website.

title: Dr Teo Keng Huat Home Care
editorial_summary:
Dr Teo Keng Huat Home Care is a residential nursing facility registered at Unit No. 50-LG1-7 & 8, Lower Ground Floor, Bangunan UOA, Jalan Dungun, Bukit Damansara — an address that places it in the heart of one of Kuala Lumpur's established professional and diplomatic enclaves, despite the facility's Selangor classification in the dataset. The home carries a physician's name, which suggests a medical founder or medical-director-led operating model, and is listed with a capacity of 30 beds. The area has convenient access to private hospitals along the Damansara corridor, including Pantai Hospital Kuala Lumpur and Damansara Specialist Hospital, which is relevant for residents who need specialist follow-up care.

Directory listings categorise Dr Teo Keng Huat Home Care as a nursing home, and its Bukit Damansara address indicates it operates in a commercial or mixed-use building — a format common among care homes in the inner Klang Valley that occupy ground or lower-ground-floor commercial lots. Specific clinical capabilities, staffing qualifications, and the range of care conditions accepted by the home are not detailed in publicly available sources. The contact number on record is 03-79568879.

Given the medically branded name and the premium catchment area, families should call to understand what clinical services distinguish this home from a standard residential care setting — particularly whether the physician name implies active medical supervision and what nursing staffing looks like overnight. Practical call-time questions include the current room types and pricing, whether the home accepts ventilator-dependent or palliative residents, and the current JKM licence status. The Bangunan UOA address in Bukit Damansara is worth confirming as the active operational address, as some directory sources list an additional Jalan Pahang address for the same provider name.

title: Felicity Nursing Home
editorial_summary:
Felicity Nursing Home is one of the longer-established private elder-care operators in Petaling Jaya, with around two decades of operation across roughly seven outlets in the Klang Valley. The PJ Old Town branch on the contact line 017-302 0285 is run by Bong Beng Choo, and the group also operates an in-house mobility-vehicle service (FNH Mobility) supplying hydraulic-ramp vans for resident transport.

The home provides 24-hour residential nursing, in-house physiotherapy delivered across all branches by a visiting physiotherapist, wound-care management, and feeding for residents requiring Ryle's tube or PEG. It also offers private nursing house calls for bedridden patients who remain at home. Branch sites are small bungalow-scale homes rather than purpose-built institutions, and online listings put the PJ Old Town site at roughly 12 beds, with reviewers noting a staff-to-resident ratio of about one staff to four or five residents.

This suits families looking for a small, household-scale home in central PJ with capability to take residents on tube feeding or with significant mobility limitations, including transport for outpatient appointments via the in-house mobility vans. The operator does not publish details on dementia-specific programming, doctor visit frequency, or licensing references, so families should confirm overnight RN cover, how acute medical episodes are escalated, what is included in the monthly fee, and whether the specific branch offered matches the one viewed.

title: IHM Nursing Care Sdn Bhd (Sunshine Home)
editorial_summary:
IHM Nursing Care Sdn Bhd operates the Sunshine Home brand across multiple Klang Valley locations; the Petaling Jaya branch sits at 80 Jalan Templer, 46000 PJ — a central address within walking distance of Assunta Hospital, which is among the most useful geographic facts about this facility. Assunta's proximity means that GP and specialist consultations, emergency transfers, and diagnostics are logistically straightforward compared with homes in more suburban corridors. The PJ Sunshine Home is a 19-bed facility offering single and double-bedded rooms, wheelchair ramps, and a garden — modest in scale but well-located for families based in central PJ.

The home provides 24-hour nursing care and, based on information from the affiliated Carey Home branch, appears to include dementia-specific programming: one documented case references physical exercises twice weekly, mind stimulation activities, and structured rest periods for a dementia resident. While this is drawn from the sister branch rather than confirmed specifically for the Jalan Templer location, it suggests the IHM group has dementia care competency across its network. The group's website (ihm.com.my) confirms the nursing home focus, though granular staffing ratios and clinical protocols are not publicly published.

Families should note that IHM Sunshine Home does not appear to use a computerised care management system, which may affect how care records and medication administration logs are tracked. Given the 19-bed ceiling, availability can be tight — enquiring about waitlist status early is sensible. Ask specifically about overnight nursing cover ratios, how high-dependency residents (PEG feeding, tracheostomy, active wound care) are managed, and whether pricing at the PJ branch differs from the Carey Home published rates.

title: Moonleaf Medicare Centre
editorial_summary:
Moonleaf Medicare Centre is a care facility in Kota Damansara, Petaling Jaya, with an address identified at No. 36, Jalan Rimba Riang 9/10, Section 9, Kota Damansara, 47810 Petaling Jaya. Kota Damansara is a well-serviced township in northern Petaling Jaya with established road links and proximity to Sunway Medical Centre Damansara and Alpha Specialist Centre, making it a practical residential location for seniors who need occasional specialist access. The facility is listed with 20 beds and is contactable at 012-9300233.

Moonleaf Medicare Centre keeps a low online profile — no dedicated website or active social-media presence has been identified in research — so confirmed details on care capabilities, nursing staff qualifications, and resident programmes are not publicly available. The "Medicare" in the name suggests a clinically oriented positioning, though whether this reflects the level of nursing cover or simply the branding choice is best clarified directly.
